claims.claim: We investigate whether explicit boundary supervision on evidence span start and end positions can reduce boundary drift compared to token-only evidence classification when adjacent distractor tokens share superficial similarity to true evidence tokens.

evidence_refs.quote: # Evidence Span Boundary Loss: Reducing Boundary Drift in Evidence Extraction via Auxiliary Start/End Supervision

claims.notes: Historical backfill linked the claim to already published artifacts; this is not independent source revalidation.

claims.claim: In a controlled synthetic benchmark where evidence tokens emit a broad membership signal and neighboring distractors partially overlap that signal, we compare a token-only logistic evidence classifier against a boundary-aware variant that adds auxiliary start/end logistic classifiers as a boundary correction term during span decoding.

evidence_refs.quote: # Evidence Span Boundary Loss: Reducing Boundary Drift in Evidence Extraction via Auxiliary Start/End Supervision

claims.notes: Historical backfill linked the claim to already published artifacts; this is not independent source revalidation.

claims.claim: Across 12 random seeds with 300 test documents each, the boundary-aware method reduces mean start absolute error by 0.72 tokens (3.38 → 2.66), end absolute error by 0.76 tokens (3.35 → 2.58), and improves exact span match from 1.4% to 13.9%.

claims.claim: This pipeline faces a known failure mode: when tokens adjacent to the true evidence boundary share surface features with evidence tokens, the token-only classifier tends to over-predict, producing spans that creep into neighboring distractor regions.
